coredata.dc:title: Extension of phosphorescence and delayed fluorescence lifetime of carbon dots using substrate-assisted method

coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Minjuan
Qiu, Huakai
Chen, Xiaoping
Wang, Xudong
Cai, Zhixiong
coredata.dc:description: Enhancing excitons utilization efficiency is critical for luminescent materials, particularly concerning the harvesting of triplet excitons. Controlling the excited-state dynamics thus plays a pivotal role in modulating room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) emissions. In this work, six similar carboxylic acids were selected as precursors to synthesize six kinds of carbon dots (CDs) via simple hydrothermal method. Subsequently, CD-based borate composites were developed through a matrix-assisted method using boric acid (BA), resulting in electron-transfer-enhanced afterglows with varying lifetimes, attributed to the inorganic electronic defects within the matrix. By fine-tuning the precursor structure in conjunction with the BA matrix, the interplay between TADF and RTP was effectively modulated. Notably, the CDs@BA composites exhibited quite different afterglow emission properties, due to the confinement effects of physical immobilization, enabling differentiation between TADF and RTP. These results provide a model for fabricating multimodal afterglow materials based on CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Long-life afterglow materials
Anti-counterfeiting
Information encryption
Room temperature phosphorescence
Delayed fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of matrix-free CDs
Preparation and characterization of CDs@BA composites
Regulation of RTP Performance through TS-FRET

The authors are grateful to the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 21904055 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian province (Grant 2023\u202F J011815 and 2020\u202F J05164 ), the Office of the President, Minnan Normal University (Grant KJ18001 and KJ2023002 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Morphological and Chemical Composition. TEM images of (a) Pht-CDs, (b) Iso-CDs, (c) Ter-CDs, (d) Hem-CDs, (e) Ben-CDs, and (f) Tri-CDs, with insets illustrating particle size distributions. XRD spectra (g), XPS spectra (h), FTIR spectra (i), UV–visible absorption spectra (j), fluorescence emission spectra (k), and fluorescence lifetime curves (l) of the six matrix-free CDs.

(a) TEM image of Iso-CDs@BA; (b) HR-TEM image of Iso-CDs@BA; (c) FTIR spectra of the six CDs@BA composites; (d) XPS high-resolution C 1 s spectra of Iso-CDs@BA; (e) High-resolution B 1 s spectra of Iso-CDs@BA; (f) XRD spectra of six CDs@BA composites.

(a-f) Transient and delayed spectra of CDs@BA afterglow materials. (g) Photographs of the afterglow colors and afterglow lifetimes of CDs@BA afterglow materials under 254 nm and 365 nm UV excitation and after the UV lamp is turned off over time.

Under 254 nm excitation. (a-c) Variable temperature delay spectra of Tri-CDs@BA, Iso-CDs@BA and Ter-CDs@BA; (d-f) Variable temperature delay lifetime spectra of Tri-CDs@BA, Iso-CDs@BA and Ter-CDs@BA (Inset: the trend plot of lifetime at different temperatures); (g-i) Variable temperature delayed lifetime spectrogram of Pht-CDs@BA, Hem-CDs@BA and Ben-CDs@BA; (j-l) Variable temperature delayed lifetime of Pht-CDs@BA, Hem-CDs@BA and Ben-CDs@BA (Inset: the trend plot of lifetime at different temperatures).

Anti-counterfeiting and information encryption applications of CD-based afterglow materials. (a) Anti-counterfeiting pattern “8888” prepared with Ben-CDs@BA; (b) Anti-counterfeiting pattern “I Believe You” produced using Pht-CDs@BA; (c) Anti-counterfeiting pattern “Pikachu” created with Hem-CDs@BA; (d, e) Anti-counterfeiting pattern “中国” utilizing Iso-CDs@BA and Ter-CDs@BA; (f, g) Anti-counterfeiting pattern “Butterfly” developed from Ter-CDs@BA; (h) Anti-counterfeiting patterns “Tiger,” “Flower with Leaves,” and “Butterfly” employing CDs-based afterglow materials enhanced by TS-FRET; (i) Morse code representation collaboratively illustrated using color-tunable afterglow materials based on TS-FRET.
Schematic illustration of the preparation process for CD-based BA composites, accompanied by afterglow color photographs of the composites before and after switching off the 254 nm UV lamp.

head.ce:title: Extension of phosphorescence and delayed fluorescence lifetime of carbon dots using substrate-assisted method

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environment Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology, Micro-Nano Organic Optical Materials Laboratory, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou, Fujian, 363000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemical Engineering and Environment Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environment Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology, Micro-Nano Organic Optical Materials Laboratory, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou, Fujian, 363000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Human Phenome Institute, Fudan University, Shanghai 200438, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Human Phenome Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Human Phenome Institute, Fudan University, Shanghai 200438, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Enhancing excitons utilization efficiency is critical for luminescent materials, particularly concerning the harvesting of triplet excitons. Controlling the excited-state dynamics thus plays a pivotal role in modulating room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) emissions. In this work, six similar carboxylic acids were selected as precursors to synthesize six kinds of carbon dots (CDs) via simple hydrothermal method. Subsequently, CD-based borate composites were developed through a matrix-assisted method using boric acid (BA), resulting in electron-transfer-enhanced afterglows with varying lifetimes, attributed to the inorganic electronic defects within the matrix. By fine-tuning the precursor structure in conjunction with the BA matrix, the interplay between TADF and RTP was effectively modulated. Notably, the CDs@BA composites exhibited quite different afterglow emission properties, due to the confinement effects of physical immobilization, enabling differentiation between TADF and RTP. These results provide a model for fabricating multimodal afterglow materials based on CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, luminescent materials exhibiting long-lived room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) have found widespread applications in areas such as bioimaging [1,2], organic light-emitting diodes [3,4], anti-counterfeiting [5–7], sensors [8], and in diverse fields. Enhancing exciton utilization efficiency is a critical scientific challenge when employing luminescent materials, particularly concerning the harvesting of triplet excitons [9,10], which could catalyze further advancements and innovations in this field. To surpass the constraints on exciton utilization efficiency, several mainstream mechanisms have been proposed, including RTP, TADF, triplet–triplet annihilation (TTA), and “hot excitons” to fully harness triplet excitons [11–13]. Among these, TADF materials utilize triplet excitons through a reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) process from the excited triplet state to the lowest excited singlet state (S1), primarily benefiting from the small energy splitting between S1 and T1 [14,15]. Meanwhile, materials based on RTP can exploit nearly 100 % of excitons by accelerated intersystem crossing (ISC) from singlet to triplet excitons. For long-lived emitting luminescent materials with larger ΔEST, RTP may take precedence over TADF [16]. Thus, controlling the excited-state dynamics plays a pivotal role in modulating RTP and TADF emissions. However, manipulation of excited-state dynamics in RTP- and TADF- based materials primarily occurs at the molecular level and within aggregates.

Most conventional RTP materials, such as inorganic or metal–organic complexes containing precious metals, are expensive and less sustainable [17–19]. In contrast, organic RTP materials are more environmentally friendly and cost-effective, but they often exhibit shorter lifetimes, involve complex synthesis, and suffer from instability. Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel class of afterglow nanomaterials, offer tunable emission wavelengths [20,21], high physicochemical inertness, good luminescence stability, low biotoxicity and simple preparation, drawing significant attention [22–24]. CDs offer unique advantages over traditional fluorescent materials due to their long-lived excitations and potential applications in biological, optoelectronic, and energy-related fields [25–30]. Recent studies on their luminescence properties reveal that enhancing spin–orbit coupling (SOC) and suppressing non-radiative decay of triplet states can significantly extend the afterglow lifetime, offering a promising pathway for the development of stable, low-cost, and eco-friendly afterglow materials [31–38]. To further advance long-afterglow CDs, it is essential to regulate their luminescence behavior, particularly in balancing between RTP and TADF.

The stabilization of excitons in the triplet state is crucial for both RTP and TADF. So far, the realization of afterglow in luminescent materials involves ISC and RISC processes, which can be effectively facilitated by heteroatom doping and substrate assistance. Co-doping of non-metal atoms (B, N, S and P, etc.) can increase the content of functional groups (e.g., C = O and C = N) in CDs, then the electron density distribution and energy gap of the CDs are altered, which enhances the SOC and effectively facilitate the ISC and RISC processes [39,40]. In addition to this, numerous studies have demonstrated that incorporating CDs into a rigid substrate matrix can successfully prolongs the life of CDs with long-afterglow effects [41–44]. Substrate-assisted methods embedding CDs into a substrate through covalent, hydrogen bonding or spatial confinement, can immobilize CDs to reduce non-radiative decay while preventing the quenching of excited triplet states by external factors like air and water. This protective strategy constructs a matrix on the CD surface, limiting vibrational and rotational energy dissipation at luminescent centers. Most CD-based afterglow materials predominantly exhibit RTP properties due to the challenges in synergistically stabilizing the excited triplet state and controlling ΔEST, limiting their potential for advanced applications. However, the dual-mode afterglow emission (RTP and TADF) of CD-based materials presents a promising alternative, given the sensitivity of these emissions to temperature, which is advantageous for various advanced applications [45–47]. Therefore, exploring CD-based afterglow materials with dual-mode emission of RTP and TADF would further promote their in-depth applications in advanced sensing [48–51], anti-counterfeiting and encryption [52–55].

In this work, we synthesized six types of CDs with RTP and TADF properties using a one-step hydrothermal method with six distinct organic carboxylic acids as precursors (Scheme 1 ). Subsequently, these CDs were incorporated into a boric acid (BA) matrix to develop afterglow materials, offering significant potential for advanced anti-counterfeiting and encryption. The BA matrix, with its electron-withdrawing boron atoms, promotes the formation of p-π conjugated systems, thereby facilitating ISC. Additionally, its rigid structure stabilizes excited triplet states. Furthermore, the interaction between oxygen vacancy electron defects in the BA matrix and the CDs further enhances afterglow emission. The six resulting afterglow materials exhibited distinct emission mechanisms and lifetimes, rendering them highly effective for applications in anti-counterfeiting and encrypted information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of matrix-free CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, six matrix-free CDs were synthesized using a modified one-step hydrothermal method, according to the literature (Detailed synthesis procedures are provided in the supporting information) [56]. Their morphology and chemical composition were characterized and compared. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 1 a–f, Fig. S1) confirmed the successful preparation of the CDs, revealing that they were well-dispersed, crystallized, and exhibited spherical shapes with uniform particle sizes. The average diameters of the CDs, Pht-CDs, Iso-CDs, Ter-CDs, Hem-CDs, Ben-CDs, and Tri-CDs, were determined to be 1.98 nm, 2.01 nm, 1.92 nm, 1.79 nm, 1.67 nm, and 2.19 nm, respectively. Further analysis using X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Fig. 1g) revealed a broad diffraction peak in the range of 18° to 35°, characteristic of amorphous carbon, confirming the amorphous nature of the synthesized CDs. Additional structural characterization was conducted using Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS full spectrum (Fig. 1h) exhibited three characteristic peaks (C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s), indicating the presence of C, N, and O in all samples. The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. S2) displayed peaks corresponding to C-N = C (399.4 eV), C-N (400.4 eV), and N-H (401.4 eV). Similarly, the resolved O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S3) was deconvoluted into two peaks attributed to C-O-C/C-OH (530.9 eV) and C = O (531.7 eV). The C 1 s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. S4) revealed contributions from C = C/C-C (284.5 eV), C-O (285.6 eV), and amide groups (N-C = O, 287.8 eV).

The FTIR spectra of the six matrix-free CDs, as depicted in Fig. 1i, exhibit notable similarities. Broad absorption bands attributed to O-H and N-H stretching vibrations are observed in the range of 3300–3700 cm−1, while C-H stretching vibrations produce broad bands from 2700 to 3300 cm−1. Furthermore, absorption peaks at 1650 and 1302 cm−1 correspond to the amide (N-C = O) and C-O bonds, respectively. Additionally, peaks at 1599 and 1429 cm−1 are associated with the bending vibrations of the N-H and C-N bonds. These results indicate the presence of polymer/carbon hybridized structures on the surface of the CDs, comprising carbonyl, amide, and other functional groups. Based on these findings, we hypothesize that the six synthesized matrix-free CDs share a highly similar structure and resemble spherical carbon nanoparticles. The core of each CDs consists of a carbon skeleton, while the surface is decorated with carbon chains and various functional groups, including C = O, N-C = O, and –OH/–NH2.

Subsequent optical characterization of the six substrate-free CDs was conducted using UV–visible absorption spectroscopy (Fig. 1j). Pht-CDs, Hem-CDs, and Ben-CDs exhibit two distinct absorption peaks at 300–360 nm and 370–460 nm, corresponding to the n → π* transitions of C = O/C = N bonds and the characteristic sp2 carbon bonding, respectively. In contrast, Iso-CDs, Ter-CDs, and Tri-CDs display a single absorption peak within the range of 300–360 nm, attributed solely to the n → π* transitions of C = O/C = N bonds. The formation of sp2 carbon bonds is due to Pht-CDs, Hem-CDs, and Ben-CDs derived CDs due to symmetrical or ortho carboxyl substitutions on the aromatic rings of their respective precursors, a structural feature that also contributes to the formation of the six-membered ring and promotes the formation of rigid, planar structures with enhanced π-conjugation. And the sp2 hybridization is also crucial for electron delocalization, contributing to the observed photophysical properties. In contrast, Iso-CDs, Ter-CDs, and Tri-CDs lack symmetrical or ortho carboxyl substitutions, resulting in less planar structural configurations and different bonding environments that do not support sp2 carbon formation. The lack of sp2 carbon bonding alters the electronic state, as evidenced by the insignificant π-π* leaps typical of sp2 carbons. Synthetic control is achieved primarily through the selection of appropriate precursors, which themselves determine the structural features of the resulting CDs [56]. As shown in Fig. 1k, the fluorescence emission peaks for Iso-CDs, Ter-CDs, and Tri-CDs are centered ∼ 450 nm, whereas Pht-CDs, Hem-CDs, and Ben-CDs exhibit emission peaks at approximately 500 nm. Additionally, fluorescence and phosphorescence lifetime measurements were conducted on the six matrix-free CD solutions. The results, presented in Fig. 1l, reveal fluorescence lifetimes of 3.74 ns, 4.47 ns, 3.03 ns, 3.61 ns, 4.11 ns, and 4.34 ns for Pht-CDs, Iso-CDs, Ter-CDs, Hem-CDs, Ben-CDs, and Tri-CDs, respectively. Notably, none of the six matrix-free CD solutions exhibited significant afterglow emission at room temperature (Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CDs@BA composites
ce:section.ce:para: Subsequently, a series of ultra-long afterglow materials were successfully synthesized, designated as CDs@BA, by embedding six different heteroatom-free CDs into a BA matrix (refer to the Supporting Information for detailed procedures), all of which exhibited significant afterglow emissions. As shown in Fig. 2 a and b, Iso-CDs@BA was selected as a representative composite for further property characterization. TEM analysis revealed that the CDs displayed excellent dispersion with uniform sizes, confirmed the successful integration of CDs within the BA matrix, thereby validating the preparation of the Iso-CDs@BA composite. To gain deeper insight, FTIR and XPS spectroscopy were utilized to further characterize its composition. The FTIR spectra of CDs@BA presented in Fig. 2c demonstrated characteristic absorption bands at 3000–3750 cm−1, corresponding to –OH stretching vibrations. Additionally, the absorption band at 1490 cm−1 was ascribed to B-O bond stretching, while the peak at 948 cm−1 indicated the presence of B-C bonds. Notably, the spectral peaks of the six CDs@BA composites largely overlapped with those of the fused boric acid, suggesting effective encapsulation of the CDs within the BA matrix.

The high-resolution C 1 s spectra of Iso-CDs@BA (Fig. 2d) revealed three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.6 eV, and 289 eV, corresponding to sp2 C, C-O, and O-C = O, respectively. The high-resolution B 1 s spectra (Fig. 2e) were fitted to three bands centered at 194.4 eV, 193.6 eV, and 192.6 eV, representing B-O, B2O3 and BCO2. Moreover, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CDs@BA also showed characteristic peaks of boron oxide and amorphous glassy state (Fig. 2f). Collectively, these results indicate that CDs were uniformly dispersed within the BA matrix and that a covalent coupling reaction occurred between CDs and BA, facilitating the introduction of electron-absorbing boron atoms and the formation of boron–carbon bonds.

The optical properties of the series of ultra-long afterglow materials, comprising Pht-CDs@BA, Iso-CDs@BA, Ter-CDs@BA, Hem-CDs@BA, Ben-CDs@BA, and Tri-CDs@BA, were systematically investigated under 254 nm and 365 nm excitation. Prior to this, we measured the excitation spectra of CDs@BA and the emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths, along with the solid-state UV–Vis absorption spectra (Figs. S6–S8), to ensure the excitation at 254 nm and 365 nm. As depicted in Fig. 3 a–f, under 254 nm excitation, the transient emission peak wavelength positions for Pht-CDs@BA, Iso-CDs@BA, Ter-CDs@BA, Hem-CDs@BA, Ben-CDs@BA and Tri-CDs@BA were found at 420 nm, 426 nm, 412 nm, 430 nm, 424 nm and 425 nm, respectively. The corresponding delayed emission peaks occurred at 465 nm, 481 nm, 464 nm, 455 nm, 476 nm, and 475 nm. Under 365 nm excitation (Fig. S9), the transient emission peaks were observed at 430 nm, 433 nm, 455 nm, 428 nm, 450 nm, and 424 nm, while the delayed emission peaks appeared at 499 nm, 491 nm, 518 nm, 495 nm, 500 nm, and 497 nm. Fig. 3g illustrates the visual appearance of these ultra-long afterglow materials under both 254 nm and 365 nm UV light irradiation, as well as the afterglow observed post-irradiation. The color of the afterglow changed over time, and the phosphorescence lifetimes of the composite materials were recorded. Under 254 nm UV light, all six CDs@BA composites displayed an indigo blue color. Upon turning off the UV light, the solid composites Pht- CDs@BA, Hem-CDs@BA, and Ben-CDs@BA retained an indigo blue color, whereas Iso-CDs@BA, Ter-CDs@BA, and Tri-CDs@BA exhibited a blue color. The visible afterglow durations for the solid powder composites were 10 s, 7 s, 5 s, 1 s, 0.5 s and 0.1 s, with corresponding remaining glow lifetimes of 2.245 s, 1.052 s, 0.874 s, 0.043 s, 0.022 s and 0.010 s. Under 365 nm UV irradiation, all composites again appeared indigo blue; after switching off the UV lamp, Pht-CDs@BA, Hem-CDs@BA and Ben-CDs@BA transitioned to a cyan color. The afterglow durations for these six solid powder composites were 10 s, 7 s, 5 s, 3 s, 1 s and 0.5 s, with remaining glow lifetimes of 2.643 s, 0.972 s, 0.835 s, 0.335 s, 0.329 s and 0.242 s, respectively. Additionally, the fluorescence quantum yields of them were evaluated under 365 nm excitation, yielding values of 24.26 %, 21.06 %, 21.70 %, 26.09 %, 17.93 % and 21.57 %, respectively (as shown in Fig. S10). Based on the analysis of peak positions gap in the transient and delayed spectra, along with the duration of their afterglow lifetimes, we hypothesize that the afterglow type of Pht-CDs@BA (Fig. 3a), Hem-CDs@BA (Fig. 3d) and Ben-CDs@BA (Fig. 3f) powders exhibit characteristics of both delayed fluorescence (DF) and RTP, while the afterglow type of Iso-CD@BA (Fig. 3b), Ter-CD@BA (Fig. 3c) and Tri-CD@BA (Fig. 3e) powders are primarily classified as RTP.

The transient (fluorescence) and delayed (phosphorescence) spectra correspond to exciton transitions from the lowest excited singlet state to the triplet state. Consequently, ΔEST can be preliminarily estimated based on the analysis of these transient and delayed spectra according to Eq. (1).
(1)
Δ
E
ST
=
1240
λ
FL
-
1240
λ
Phos
Where λFL and λPhos are fluorescence and phosphorescence wavelengths, respectively. The ΔEST values for the composite materials Tri-CDs@BA, Ter-CDs@BA, Iso-CDs@BA, Hem-CDs@BA, Pht-CDs@BA, and Ben-CDs@BA were determined to be 0.307 eV, 0.338 eV, 0.333 eV, 0.158 eV, 0.286 eV, and 0.292 eV, respectively. These values, ranging from 0.15 to 0.35 eV, are indicative of effective ISC and enhanced spin–orbit coupling, which promote the generation of excitons in the triplet state and facilitate phosphorescence. The large ΔEST is not conducive to the occurrence of RISC. Therefore, the afterglow types of Iso-CD@BA, Ter-CD@BA, and Tri-CD@BA powders belong to RTP. When ΔEST is sufficiently small, excitons at T1 state can transition back to S1 state, resulting in TADF through RISC. Notably, the composites Hem-CDs@BA, Pht-CDs@BA, and Ben-CDs@BA, which possess ΔEST values below 0.3 eV, demonstrate considerable potential for ISC and RISC processes between the singlet state and the triplet state. This characteristic facilitates both TADF and RTP. Consequently, this phenomenon accounts for the shorter lifetimes observed in Hem-CDs@BA, Pht-CDs@BA and Ben-CDs@BA. CDs with a more rigid structure and enhanced spin–orbit coupling, possibly facilitated by functional groups, exhibit longer triplet state lifetimes, which promotes RTP. CDs displaying RTP modes are mainly dependent on triplet state stabilization and slower non-radiative decay processes. The lack of sp2 carbon bonding in these composites results in lower RISC efficiency, favoring RTP over TADF.

Considering that the RISC process directly influences the lifetime of delayed fluorescence, the ΔEST can be derived from the rate constant of RISC (kRISC ). The relationship between kRISC and ΔEST is expressed in Eq. (2) [57].
(2)
k
RISC
= A e x p
( -
Δ E
ST
k B
T
)

To further elucidate the afterglow mechanism of the composite materials, we conducted variable temperature tests ranging from 77 to 317 K, as illustrated in Fig. 4 . According to Eq. (2), we derived the functional relationship between kRISC and temperature. ΔEST is a crucial determinant of the afterglow type for these materials. Boltzmann statistics indicate that a smaller ΔEST facilitates the occurrence of RISC, particularly when ΔEST is less than 0.3 eV. For the Hem-CDs@BA, Pht-CDs@BA, and Ben-CDs@BA composites, both afterglow intensity (Fig. 4g–i) and lifetime (Fig. 4j–l) increased with rising temperature, a hallmark of typical TADF. In contrast, the Tri-CDs@BA, Ter-CDs@BA, and Iso-CDs@BA composites, characterized by ΔEST values greater than 0.3 eV, exhibited a significant decrease in both afterglow intensity (Fig. 4a–c) and lifetime (Fig. 4d–f) with increasing temperature, confirming their predominantly phosphorescent nature. This observation aligns with the UV–Vis absorption data (Fig. 1j), where Pht-CDs, Hem-CDs, and Ben-CDs displayed distinct absorption peaks in the range of 370–460 nm, indicative of characteristic sp2 carbon bonding, which was absent in the Tri-CDs, Ter-CDs, and Iso-CDs. The presence of sp2 carbon bonding imparts a more rigid structure to the prepared CDs, thereby contributing to the smaller ΔEST values observed in Pht-CDs, Hem-CDs, and Ben-CDs.

The presence of sp2 carbon bonds helps to stabilize these excited states, which reduces the ΔEST and promotes TADF. The small ΔEST not only comes from the sp2 carbon bonds, but also from the rigidity of π-conjugation, functional groups [56], and the rigidity of the CDs that influence the overall electronic structure [58]. Functional groups like carbonyls and hydroxyls can introduce additional energy levels within the band gap, affecting the emission properties and promoting non-radiative leaps. The overall molecular structure, including the degree of π-conjugation and the arrangement of functional groups, plays an important role in determining the electronic properties and luminescent behavior of CDs. Experimental results show that the structural properties of Pht-CDs, Hem-CDs and Ben-CDs are intrinsically correlated with their TADF and RTP behaviors. The small ΔEST values, favorable temperature effects on luminescence properties [59], and the unique combination of phosphorescence and TADF together indicate significant advantages for these three CDs.

The unique afterglow properties of CDs@BA composite materials, characterized by diverse lifetimes and stability, present significant potential for multi-level information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications. As illustrated in Fig. 4a and Fig. S14, we demonstrated this capability by creating the digital sequence “8888” using two materials with distinct properties: Ben-CDs@BA powder and BA monomer solid powder, which were selectively filled in different zones. Under 254 nm UV illumination, the bright indigo blue numeral “8888” is displayed, initially revealing incorrect encryption information. Upon turning off the UV lamp, the correct encryption information is revealed as a bright blue afterglow, displaying “6699”. Similarly, under 365 nm UV excitation, the numeral “8888” again presents in bright blue, indicating incorrect encryption. When the 365 nm UV light is extinguished, the accurate encryption appears as a green phosphorescent numeral “6699”.

In Fig. 5 b and Fig. S15, we applied different materials to convey the phrase “I Believe You”. The letters “I”, “lie” and “you” were filled with solid powder Pht-CDs@BA, while “be” and “ve” utilized BA monomer solid powder. When illuminated with both 254 nm and 365 nm UV lamps, the entire phrase displayed a bright blue color. After turn off the 254 nm UV lamp, the phrase transitioned to exhibit a blue afterglow as “I lie you”. Following the cessation of 365 nm UV illumination, the afterglow shifted to green. This variation in afterglow properties and their corresponding lifetimes enables a transformation from “I believe you” to “I lie you”, effectively illustrating the potential for dynamic information encoding. As show in Fig. 5c and Fig. S16, Hem-CDs@BA is employed for pattern filling, resulting in the “Pikachu” design exhibiting a blue color under both 254 nm and 365 nm UV lamps. Upon deactivation of the 254 nm UV lamp, the “Pikachu” pattern continues to display a blue afterglow, while the cessation of the 365 nm UV lamp reveals a green color. These variations in pattern and color before and after the illumination with 254 nm and 365 nm UV light enhance the message encryption process.

Subsequently, Iso-CDs@BA and Ter-CDs@BA composites are utilized to fill different areas of the “中国” pattern, with Iso-CDs@BA used for the letters “hello” and Ter-CDs@BA for the remainder of the pattern. Under the 254 nm UV lamp, the “中国” pattern appears blue; after turning the light off, it transitions to a blue afterglow. Following a duration of 7 s, due to the differing phosphorescence lifetimes and luminescence properties of the materials, the “中国” pattern morphs into “hello”, gradually darkening until it disappears after 10 s (as shown in Fig. 5d and S17). Under the 365 nm UV lamp, the “中国” pattern shines brightly blue, shifting to green upon deactivation of the lamp, and likewise transforms into “hello” after 7 s. The pattern then continues to darken until it vanishes after 10 s (as illustrated in Fig. 5e).

Finally, Ter-CDs@BA is used to fill a butterfly pattern. Under 254 nm UV light, the “butterfly” displays a bright blue color, which persists upon turning off the UV light. Over time, the pattern gradually darkens, lasting for 7 s (Fig. 5f and S18). In contrast, under the 365 nm UV lamp, the “butterfly” also appears bright blue, but shifts to green after the lamp is turned off. As time progresses, this pattern similarly darkens until it disappears, also lasting for 7 s (Fig. 5g). The integration of CD-based RTP materials, characterized by distinct delay fluorescence emissions and afterglow durations, facilitates a dynamic anti-counterfeiting process, significantly enhancing the security of such applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Regulation of RTP Performance through TS-FRET
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to the previously mentioned regulation strategies, we implemented an efficient triplet-to-singlet Förster resonance energy transfer (TS-FRET) approach to modulate afterglow color for diverse applications [6,60]. The doping systems, exhibiting blue RTP under 254 nm excitation, were utilized as energy donors to impart persistent luminescence to organic fluorescent dyes via TS-FRET. According to the fundamental principles of FRET, effective spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of the energy donor and the absorption spectrum of the energy acceptor is essential for successful TS-FRET. We selected two commercially available water-soluble fluorescent dyes-fluorescein (Fluo) and rhodamine B (RhB)-as energy acceptors due to their significant spectral overlap with the phosphorescence of the doped system (Figs. S11 and S12). Furthermore, we posited that these co-doped systems would exhibit color-tunable and time-dependent afterglows when the doping concentration is systematically varied (Fig. S13). This capability enables the preparation of various encryption modes, thereby highlighting the potential of these materials in advanced anti-counterfeiting technologies.

Initially, we employed Ben-CDs@Fluo@BA to create the “tiger” pattern (left in Fig. 5h and S19). Under excitation with a 254 nm UV lamp, the “tiger” pattern appeared yellow; upon deactivation of the UV lamp, the color transitioned from yellow to light green, with the afterglow persisting for approximately 1 s. Subsequently, we used Tri-CDs@RhB@BA and Tri-CDs@Fluo@BA composites to depict the anti-counterfeiting motif “Flower”. Under 254 nm UV light, the pattern revealed a red “flower” and green “leaves”. When the UV lamp was turned off, the flower transitioned from red to pink, while the leaves shifted from green to navy blue. The color of the “Flower” pattern gradually changed over time, ultimately disappearing after about 5 s (middle in Fig. 5h and S20). Next, we utilized Iso-CDs@RhB@BA solid composites to fill the “Butterfly” pattern. Under the excitation of the 254 nm UV lamp, a red butterfly pattern became evident. After the UV lamp was turned off, the butterfly transitioned to a pink hue and gradually faded over approximately 8 s (right in Fig. 5h and S21).

Then, we mapped the encrypted Morse code using the color-tunable afterglow materials (Fig. 5i). The encrypted Morse code was initially constructed with Iso-CDs@RhB@BA and Iso-CDs@Fluo@BA. Under 254 nm UV excitation, the encrypted message appeared as “2024” with an afterglow duration of up to 8 s. Subsequently, we employed Iso-CDs@BA, Iso-CDs@RhB@BA, Tri-CDs@BA, Tri-CDs@RhB@BA and Hem-CDs@BA for Morse code encryption applications. When the 254 nm UV lamp was illuminated, an invalid code was displayed; however, upon deactivation of the UV lamp, the authentic code emerged as “Wife”. This encrypted message subsequently transformed into “We” after 6 s, lasting approximately 2 s. This dual-layer encryption process significantly enhances the security of the transmitted information.

In summary, we successfully fabricated a series of solid-state afterglow materials exhibiting dual emission by embedding six distinct CDs within a BA matrix. The resulting CDs@BA composites demonstrated a bimodal afterglow characterized by both RTP and TADF. The highly rigid BA matrix effectively restricts the motion and vibration of the CDs, thereby minimizing nonradiative transitions of triplet excitons. Additionally, the interactions between the CDs and the BA matrix facilitate ISC and RISC, which prolong the phosphorescence and delayed fluorescence lifetimes in the CDs@BA composites. Notably, we achieved tunable afterglow colors and time-dependent emissions through efficient TS-FRET between energy donors and acceptors. Furthermore, these composites have been successfully applied to information encryption and anti-counterfeiting technologies. These findings contribute a novel approach to engineering singlet–triplet splitting in CDs, facilitating the development of advanced multimodal afterglow materials.

Minjuan Cai: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Huakai Qiu: Visualization, Methodology, Data curation. Xiaoping Chen: Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Xudong Wang: Validation, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Zhixiong Cai: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 21904055), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian province (Grant 2023J011815 and 2024J1806), the Office of the President, Minnan Normal University (Grant KJ18001 and KJ2023002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.158747.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
